pathophysiology_summary: Impetigo is a superficial skin infection by Staphylococcus aureus and/or Streptococcus pyogenes, typically starting at a breach (here nasal trauma during a cold) and producing thin-walled vesicles/pustules that rupture to leave characteristic golden 'honey-coloured' adherent crusts on an erythematous base; it is highly contagious and spreads by autoinoculation and fomites. It is benign but the OTC hydrocortisone has worsened it (local immunosuppression). Management depends on extent: localised disease responds to topical antibiotic (mupirocin) with crust removal, while more extensive/multi-site facial impetigo warrants oral anti-staphylococcal/streptococcal antibiotics. The ground-truth tasks are to (1) recognise impetigo and treat by extent, (2) stop the topical steroid, (3) institute strict contagion control (contagious until 24h of antibiotics/crust resolution; wash linen; no sharing), and (4) safety-net the complications: spreading cellulitis/sepsis, and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (dark/cola urine 1–3 weeks later); plus exclude the mimics HSV/eczema herpeticum.

differential.evidence_basis: Honey-crusted spreading facial lesions after nasal trauma with a cold.
Screen for grouped vesicles/eczema base and systemic toxicity — would change to antiviral.
Screen for deep hot spreading painful erythema, fever — needs systemic antibiotics ± ED.
Honey-crusting and regional nodes indicate infection rather than allergy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Impetigo leads; HSV/eczema herpeticum and cellulitis must be excluded before topical/oral antibiotic choice.

differential.evidence_basis: Honey crusts, superficial, non-toxic, no deep spreading cellulitis, no eczema/vesicular HSV pattern — non-bullous impetigo; extent (multi-site facial) favours oral antibiotics.
No deep hot painful spreading erythema/fever; escalation target if it develops.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative cellulitis/HSV screen, impetigo leads; treat by extent, stop the steroid, contagion control, PSGN safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; heals in 7–10 days on antibiotics with contagion control.

red_flags.description: Cellulitis/systemic infection — deep hot spreading painful erythema, high fever/rigors, periorbital swelling
Eczema herpeticum / severe HSV — widespread monomorphic punched-out lesions on eczema, systemic toxicity
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis — dark/cola-coloured urine, facial/leg swelling 1–3 weeks after infection
red_flags.clinical_significance: A recognised late complication of streptococcal impetigo.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not stopping the OTC hydrocortisone, which suppresses local immunity and accelerates bacterial spread.
Relying on topical antibiotic alone for multi-site facial spread that warrants oral antibiotics.
Not warning about dark urine weeks later.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of regional lymph nodes and assessment of deep erythema/induration
Full skin examination for extent/other sites
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Treat by extent (topical mupirocin if localised; oral antibiotic if extensive/multi-site facial), stop the steroid, contagion control; ED for cellulitis/sepsis features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing cellulitis/eczema herpeticum; under-treating extensive disease.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Localised impetigo: topical mupirocin after crust removal. Multiple lesions/extensive/facial: oral di/flucloxacillin (or cephalexin; consider streptococcal cover). Advise contagion control (contagious until 24h of antibiotics or crusts resolved). Watch for post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.
